3 min read
Just now

--

Every few months there’s a new headline claiming AI is about to make developers obsolete. I’ve read enough of them now to roll my eyes a little. But I’ll be honest — when GitHub Copilot first showed up in my editor, I did feel a small knot of anxiety. If a machine can write my code, what exactly am I here for?
A couple of years and a lot of AI-assisted commits later, I have a much calmer answer: AI didn’t replace me. It changed what I actually spend my day doing. And once you understand how it’s changing things, the panic mostly goes away.
The Job Didn’t Disappear — It Moved
Here’s what nobody tells you when the scary headlines drop: AI is really good at the parts of coding that were never the hard part to begin with.
Writing a boilerplate component? AI can spit that out in seconds. Need a regex you’ll forget in a week anyway? AI’s got it. Stuck on why your CSS isn’t centering a div for the fortieth time in your career? AI will tell you before you even finish typing the question.
None of that is the skill. The skill was never “I can type syntax.” The skill is knowing what to build, why it should work that way, and whether the thing AI just handed you is actually correct, secure, and maintainable. That part hasn’t gone anywhere. If anything, it’s become more valuable, because someone still has to catch the mistakes.
Where AI Actually Helps Day to Day
In practice, here’s where AI has quietly become part of a normal workflow:
Debugging gets faster because you can paste an error and get a plausible explanation instantly, instead of digging through five Stack Overflow tabs. Boilerplate and repetitive setup — forms, API scaffolding, test stubs — go from a tedious hour to a few minutes. Documentation, which almost every developer avoids writing, becomes far less painful when AI can draft the first pass. Even code review benefits, since AI can flag obvious issues before a human reviewer has to.
None of this replaces judgment. It just clears out the noise so you can spend more time on the decisions that actually matter.
Why “AI Will Replace Developers” Misses the Point
The fear usually comes from picturing AI as a finished product that writes an entire application correctly from a single prompt. That’s not what’s actually happening in real projects.
AI-generated code still needs someone who understands the system to review it, fix subtle bugs, make architectural calls, and take responsibility when something breaks in production. AI doesn’t know your business logic, your users, or the five edge cases that only exist because of a decision made two years ago. It doesn’t sit in on planning meetings. It doesn’t feel the weight of shipping something broken to real customers.
Developers who lean into AI as a tool are moving faster. Developers who ignore it are working harder than they need to. But the developers actually at risk aren’t the ones being “replaced” — they’re the ones who never learned to evaluate the code they’re shipping in the first place, AI-written or not.
What This Means If You’re Just Starting Out
If you’re early in your career, this is worth hearing clearly: don’t skip the fundamentals because AI can generate the answer. Understanding why code works is what lets you catch it when AI is confidently wrong — and it will be wrong, more often than the hype suggests.
Use AI to move faster on the boring parts. Don’t use it as a substitute for actually learning how the web works.
The Real Shift
AI isn’t taking over web development. It’s raising the floor for what “basic” output looks like, and pushing the real value of a developer higher up the stack — toward judgment, architecture, and understanding the why behind the code.
That’s not something to panic about. It’s honestly a pretty good trade.
